items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
items.correct: Mass is the amount of matter (kg); weight is the gravitational force on that mass (N) and depends on g.
items.misconception: Mass and weight are the same thing, just expressed in different units.
items.random_wrong: Mass changes with location but weight does not.
items.why: Students confuse mass with weight because everyday English uses 'weight' for both, and bathroom scales display mass in kg labelled as 'weight'.

items.q: If air resistance is ignored, which falls faster: a heavy ball or a light ball dropped from the same height?
items.correct: Both fall at the same rate because gravitational acceleration is independent of mass.
items.misconception: Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects because gravity pulls more on them.
items.random_wrong: The lighter ball falls faster because it has less inertia.
items.why: Students reason 'more gravity force = more acceleration' but forget the heavier object also has proportionally more inertia, cancelling out.

items.q: For a projectile launched horizontally, what affects the horizontal component of velocity during flight (ignoring air resistance)?
items.correct: Nothing — horizontal velocity stays constant; only vertical velocity changes due to gravity.
items.misconception: Gravity slows down both the vertical and horizontal motion.
items.random_wrong: The horizontal velocity increases over time because the projectile is falling.
items.why: Students treat 'gravity affects all motion' too broadly, missing that gravity acts only vertically and the components are independent.

items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.

items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.

items.q: What property of a sound wave determines its pitch?
items.correct: The frequency — higher frequency means higher pitch.
items.misconception: Amplitude determines pitch — a bigger wave is a higher-pitched note.
items.random_wrong: The speed of the wave determines pitch — faster waves sound higher.
items.why: Students conflate the two main wave properties: amplitude relates to volume, frequency to pitch, but they often swap them.

items.q: How do you see a non-luminous object like a book?
items.correct: Light from a source reflects off the book and enters your eyes.
items.misconception: Your eyes send out beams of vision that hit the book and let you see it.
items.random_wrong: The book emits a special invisible signal that your brain detects directly.
items.why: The 'extramission' model of vision is a stubborn childhood intuition, reinforced by phrases like 'cast your eye over' or comic-book vision rays.

items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: What does voltage (potential difference) across a component represent?
items.correct: The energy transferred per unit charge passing through that component.
items.misconception: Voltage is the 'amount of electricity' flowing through the wire.
items.random_wrong: Voltage measures how many electrons are stored in a battery.
items.why: Students conflate voltage with current because both are 'something to do with electricity'; they don't distinguish 'energy per charge' from 'rate of charge flow'.

items.q: What happens to the total resistance when you add another resistor in parallel?
items.correct: Total resistance decreases — there are more paths for current to flow.
items.misconception: Adding any resistor always increases the total resistance.
items.random_wrong: Adding a parallel resistor doubles the voltage available.
items.why: Students apply the series rule (resistances add) to parallel circuits because they don't visualise multiple paths reducing the overall opposition.

items.q: What is required to induce an emf in a coil using a magnet?
items.correct: A changing magnetic flux linkage through the coil — the magnet (or coil) must be moving or the field must be changing.
items.misconception: Just having a magnet near the coil induces an emf, even when both are stationary.
items.random_wrong: A current in the coil is what induces an emf in the magnet.
items.why: Students remember 'magnet + coil = electricity' but miss that the field must be changing for an emf to be induced (Faraday's law).

items.q: In what direction does a magnetic field exert a force on a current-carrying wire?
items.correct: Perpendicular to both the current direction and the magnetic field direction (Fleming's left hand rule).
items.misconception: The force is in the same direction as the magnetic field.
items.random_wrong: The force is in the same direction as the current in the wire.
items.why: Students expect forces to act 'along' visible directions; the perpendicular result is counterintuitive without a vector cross product mental model.

items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.

items.q: What is the difference between nuclear fission and fusion?
items.correct: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller ones; fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one.
items.misconception: Fission and fusion are the same process, just at different scales.
items.random_wrong: Fission combines small atoms into bigger ones, and fusion splits them.
items.why: The words sound similar (both start 'fi-'), and students mix up which one combines and which one splits.

items.q: Which type of nuclear radiation is most ionising?
items.correct: Alpha radiation — large, doubly charged particles cause the most ionisation per unit distance.
items.misconception: Gamma is most ionising because it can travel furthest and pass through almost anything.
items.random_wrong: Beta radiation is most ionising because it consists of fast electrons.
items.why: Students conflate penetrating power with ionising power — gamma penetrates most because it ionises least, the opposite of intuition.

items.q: Which of the following is a fundamental particle: proton, electron, or neutron?
items.correct: Only the electron — protons and neutrons are made of quarks.
items.misconception: All three (proton, electron, neutron) are fundamental particles that cannot be broken down.
items.random_wrong: Only the proton is fundamental; electrons and neutrons are composite.
items.why: The atomic model is introduced with proton/neutron/electron as building blocks, so students don't update when quarks appear in later courses.

items.q: If the distance between two masses doubles, what happens to the gravitational force between them?
items.correct: It quarters — gravitational force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
items.misconception: It halves — gravitational force is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It doubles — more distance means more space for gravity to act through.
items.why: Students default to linear relationships and forget the inverse-square law; they apply 1/r reasoning instead of 1/r^2.

items.q: Why is gravitational potential energy near a planet treated as negative?
items.correct: Because the zero is defined at infinity; objects bound to the planet have lower (negative) potential energy than at infinity.
items.misconception: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity always points downwards.
items.random_wrong: Gravitational potential energy is negative because gravity does negative work.
items.why: Students confuse the negative sign as 'direction' rather than understanding it as a consequence of choosing infinity as the zero point.

items.q: What determines the energy of a single photon?
items.correct: Its frequency (E = hf) — only frequency, not intensity.
items.misconception: A brighter light has higher-energy photons because intensity makes each photon more energetic.
items.random_wrong: Photon energy depends on how fast the photon is moving relative to the source.
items.why: Students conflate intensity (number of photons) with photon energy (frequency-dependent); this is why dim UV is more dangerous than bright red.

items.q: Why does increasing the intensity of light below the threshold frequency not eject electrons?
items.correct: Each photon's energy is below the work function; many low-energy photons can't combine to eject an electron.
items.misconception: Enough low-frequency photons should eventually eject an electron if you wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Low-frequency light doesn't have enough mass to push electrons out of the metal.
items.why: Students think energy is cumulative (more photons = more energy delivered), missing that each electron interacts with one photon at a time in the photoelectric effect.

items.q: Why do brighter-looking stars not necessarily have higher luminosity?
items.correct: Apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance — a closer dim star can look brighter than a distant luminous one.
items.misconception: Brighter-looking stars are always intrinsically more luminous.
items.random_wrong: Apparent brightness depends only on the star's temperature.
items.why: Students don't naturally factor in distance; they assume 'looks bright' means 'is bright', missing the inverse-square law applied to flux.

items.q: What does the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies indicate?
items.correct: Space itself is expanding, stretching the wavelength of light from distant galaxies as it travels.
items.misconception: Galaxies are moving through space away from us, like objects flying outward from a centre.
items.random_wrong: The galaxies are turning red because they are getting older.
items.why: Students apply the Doppler effect literally; the expansion of space is a more subtle picture they often miss.

items.q: What is the difference between heat and temperature?
items.correct: Heat is the energy transferred due to temperature difference; temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles.
items.misconception: Heat and temperature are the same thing — both measure how hot something is.
items.random_wrong: Heat is measured in degrees Celsius and temperature in Joules.
items.why: Everyday language uses 'heat' and 'temperature' interchangeably ('turn up the heat'), so students don't distinguish them physically.

items.q: Why does a metal spoon in hot water heat up faster than a wooden one?
items.correct: Metals have higher thermal conductivity, so heat is transferred more rapidly through them.
items.misconception: Metal heats up faster because it has a lower specific heat capacity, requiring less energy per degree.
items.random_wrong: Metal heats up faster because it has more thermal energy to begin with.
items.why: Students confuse the rate of heat transfer (conductivity) with how much energy a material can hold (heat capacity).

items.q: What does Young's modulus measure?
items.correct: The stiffness of a material — the ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region.
items.misconception: Young's modulus measures how strong a material is — how much force it can take before breaking.
items.random_wrong: Young's modulus measures the density of a material.
items.why: Students conflate stiffness (resistance to deformation) with strength (resistance to failure) because both feel like 'tough'.

items.q: What force keeps a car moving in a circle around a bend?
items.correct: Friction from the road acting towards the centre of the circle — centripetal force.
items.misconception: A centrifugal force pushes the car outwards from the centre.
items.random_wrong: The car's own momentum acts as a force that drives it in a circle.
items.why: Students experience the 'outward push' in a car (a result of inertia in a rotating frame) and interpret it as a real outward force.

items.q: In a collision between two objects, what quantity is always conserved (in a closed system)?
items.correct: Total momentum is always conserved; kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions.
items.misconception: Kinetic energy is always conserved in collisions because energy is conserved.
items.random_wrong: Velocity is always conserved in any collision.
items.why: Students conflate momentum conservation with energy conservation; they overlook that energy can become heat/sound in inelastic collisions.

items.q: When two waves interfere destructively, what happens to the energy?
items.correct: Energy is redistributed — there is constructive interference elsewhere, conserving total energy.
items.misconception: Energy is destroyed at points of destructive interference.
items.random_wrong: Energy turns into mass according to E=mc^2 when waves cancel.
items.why: Students take 'destructive' literally and assume energy is annihilated, missing that interference always involves a redistribution pattern.

items.q: When you rub a balloon and stick it to the wall, what has been transferred?
items.correct: Electrons have been transferred between the balloon and the cloth (not the wall).
items.misconception: Protons jumped from the balloon to the wall, causing static cling.
items.random_wrong: Charge was created by friction and pulled the balloon to the wall.
items.why: Students don't know that nuclei (and protons) are essentially immobile in everyday situations — only electrons move in static electricity.

items.q: After one half-life, what happens to an individual radioactive nucleus?
items.correct: It has a 50 percent chance of having decayed — radioactive decay is random for any single nucleus.
items.misconception: Every nucleus is guaranteed to have half-decayed after one half-life.
items.random_wrong: The nucleus shrinks to half its original size after one half-life.
items.why: Students treat half-life as deterministic for individual atoms, when it is a statistical statement about large populations of nuclei.

items.q: Can a device ever be 100 percent efficient?
items.correct: In practice, no — some energy is always transferred to less useful stores (e.g. heat to surroundings).
items.misconception: A well-designed device can be 100 percent efficient by eliminating all friction.
items.random_wrong: Efficiency above 100 percent is possible if the device extracts ambient energy.
items.why: Students think 'inefficiency' is a design flaw to be removed, missing that the second law of thermodynamics ensures some dissipation.

items.q: If a car has negative acceleration, what does that necessarily mean?
items.correct: Its acceleration is in the negative direction (often opposite to the chosen positive direction); it may be slowing down or speeding up in reverse.
items.misconception: Negative acceleration always means the car is slowing down.
items.random_wrong: Negative acceleration means the car is moving backwards.
items.why: Students conflate 'deceleration' with 'negative acceleration'; sign indicates direction relative to the chosen axis, not necessarily slowing.

items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.

items.q: What is the difference between the activity of a source and the dose received by a person?
items.correct: Activity (Bq) is the rate of nuclear decays in the source; dose (Sv) measures the biological effect of absorbed radiation on tissue.
items.misconception: Activity and dose are the same — both measure how dangerous the radiation is.
items.random_wrong: Activity is for nuclear power stations and dose is for medical X-rays.
items.why: Students conflate 'how much radiation is emitted' with 'how much radiation a person absorbs', missing the role of distance, shielding and tissue sensitivity.

items.q: Is radioactive decay periodic — does each nucleus decay at regular intervals?
items.correct: No — decay is a random process; half-life is a statistical property of a large population.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus 'ticks' at intervals of one half-life until it decays.
items.random_wrong: Yes — half-life is the time between each emission from the source.
items.why: Students treat half-life as a clock for individual atoms instead of a probability-based property of bulk samples.

items.q: Why is nuclear fusion much harder to achieve on Earth than fission?
items.correct: Fusion requires extremely high temperatures and pressures to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between positive nuclei.
items.misconception: Fusion is harder because the materials are rarer than uranium.
items.random_wrong: Fusion is harder because you need to break atoms apart first.
items.why: Students don't appreciate the Coulomb barrier; they assume the challenge is technological rather than fundamental physics.

items.q: In a balanced nuclear equation, what must be equal on both sides?
items.correct: The total mass number (top numbers) and the total atomic number (bottom numbers) must both balance.
items.misconception: Only the atomic number (proton count) needs to balance.
items.random_wrong: Only the total mass in kilograms needs to balance.
items.why: Students transfer 'balance the atoms' from chemical equations, but in nuclear equations identities can change while nucleon and charge counts are conserved.

items.q: Is visible light ionising radiation?
items.correct: No — its photon energy is below the threshold required to ionise atoms; ionising radiation includes UV (high energy), X-rays and gamma.
items.misconception: Yes — all electromagnetic radiation is ionising because it carries energy.
items.random_wrong: Yes — only infrared and radio waves are non-ionising.
items.why: Students don't link photon energy (E = hf) to the ability to ionise, so they treat all EM radiation as equivalent in damage potential.

items.q: If one EM wave has a higher frequency than another, what is true about its wavelength?
items.correct: It is shorter — wavelength and frequency are inversely related (c = f x lambda).
items.misconception: It is also longer, because both increase together.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged — only the speed differs.
items.why: Students treat all wave properties as moving together (more of one = more of the other), missing the fixed-speed constraint that ties them inversely.

items.q: Is ultraviolet light visible to humans?
items.correct: No — even though it sits next to violet on the spectrum, its frequency is above the range our eyes detect.
items.misconception: Yes — UV is visible because it's right next to violet.
items.random_wrong: Yes — UV is the colour purple.
items.why: Students assume adjacency on the EM spectrum implies visibility; they don't appreciate the sharp biological cut-off at around 400 nm.

items.q: Which has the longer wavelength: microwaves or radio waves?
items.correct: Radio waves — they sit at the longest-wavelength end of the EM spectrum.
items.misconception: Microwaves, because they are used in 'big' devices like radar.
items.random_wrong: They have the same wavelength because both are used for communication.
items.why: Students remember 'micro' as 'small' and assume the wavelength is the smallest, missing that 'micro' refers historically to the wavelength relative to radio, not to absolute size.

items.q: Why are infrared cameras used to detect warm objects in the dark?
items.correct: Warm objects emit infrared radiation due to their temperature; the camera detects this emission.
items.misconception: Infrared cameras shine infrared light onto objects so they reflect back visibly.
items.random_wrong: Infrared cameras detect heat moving through the air around objects.
items.why: Students assume cameras always work by reflection (like visible-light cameras), missing that thermal cameras passively detect emitted IR.

items.q: Why does light bend when it enters a denser medium like glass?
items.correct: Because it slows down — the change in speed at the boundary causes the wavefront to bend (towards the normal).
items.misconception: Because the medium is denser, it physically blocks part of the light and forces it to turn.
items.random_wrong: Because gravity in the glass pulls the light down.
items.why: Students attach refraction to 'density' as a material property without linking it to the change in wave speed and Snell's law.

items.q: What condition must be met for total internal reflection to occur?
items.correct: Light must travel from a denser to a less dense medium AND strike the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle.
items.misconception: Any angle is fine as long as the light is going into a less dense medium.
items.random_wrong: Light must go from a less dense to a denser medium.
items.why: Students remember 'denser to less dense' but forget the angle condition, or forget the direction requirement entirely.

items.q: What does a concave lens do to parallel rays of light?
items.correct: It diverges them — they spread out as if from a virtual focal point behind the lens.
items.misconception: It converges them to a focal point in front of the lens.
items.random_wrong: It reflects them back the way they came.
items.why: Students mix up convex (converging) and concave (diverging) lenses because the names are easy to confuse and the diagrams look similar.

items.q: What is the key difference between a real image and a virtual image?
items.correct: A real image is formed where light rays actually converge and can be projected on a screen; a virtual image is where rays only appear to come from and cannot be projected.
items.misconception: A real image is solid and a virtual image is transparent.
items.random_wrong: A real image is in colour and a virtual image is black and white.
items.why: Students attach everyday meanings to 'real' and 'virtual' and miss the technical criterion involving where light rays actually meet.

items.q: According to Faraday's law, what determines the size of the induced emf in a coil?
items.correct: The rate of change of magnetic flux linkage through the coil.
items.misconception: The strength of the magnetic field alone.
items.random_wrong: The number of turns in the coil alone.
items.why: Students focus on the field strength or the coil's properties in isolation and miss that it is the rate of change of flux linkage that matters.

items.q: Why does Faraday's law include a minus sign (Lenz's law)?
items.correct: The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that produced it — a consequence of energy conservation.
items.misconception: The minus sign is a mathematical convention with no physical meaning.
items.random_wrong: The minus sign means the current flows backwards through the wire.
items.why: Students treat the minus sign as a notational quirk rather than encoding Lenz's law, the deep statement that induced effects oppose their cause.

items.q: What is the difference between the motor effect and electromagnetic induction?
items.correct: The motor effect produces a force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field; induction produces an emf when flux linkage changes.
items.misconception: They are the same phenomenon described in different words.
items.random_wrong: The motor effect only works on DC; induction only works on AC.
items.why: Students see both involve coils, magnets and electricity and don't separate 'current causes force' (motor) from 'changing flux causes emf' (induction).

items.q: Why do transformers only work with alternating current?
items.correct: A transformer needs a changing magnetic flux in the core; DC produces a steady flux and so no emf is induced in the secondary.
items.misconception: Transformers work with DC too, just less efficiently.
items.random_wrong: DC cannot enter a transformer because the iron core blocks it.
items.why: Students remember transformers as boxes that 'change voltage' and don't link the operation to changing flux being a requirement.

items.q: What does the spacing of field lines on a diagram indicate?
items.correct: The strength of the field — closer lines mean a stronger field.
items.misconception: Field lines are equally important regardless of spacing — only the arrows matter.
items.random_wrong: Wider spacing means a stronger field because the lines have more room to act.
items.why: Students treat field lines as a count rather than a density-based representation; they miss that the convention encodes strength via spacing.

items.q: What does gravitational field strength at a point represent?
items.correct: The force per unit mass that would act on a test mass placed at that point.
items.misconception: The total gravitational force at that point regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: The mass of the object creating the field.
items.why: Students forget the 'per unit mass' (or per unit charge) part of field strength definitions, conflating the field with the resulting force.

items.q: Why is the electric field between parallel charged plates considered uniform?
items.correct: Field strength is constant in magnitude and direction throughout the region (away from edges), unlike the inverse-square field of a point charge.
items.misconception: The field still decreases with the inverse square of distance — uniform just means it's smooth.
items.random_wrong: Because the plates are uniform in shape.
items.why: Students extrapolate inverse-square thinking everywhere; they don't distinguish point sources from extended geometries.

items.q: What produces a magnetic field around a wire?
items.correct: A moving electric charge — i.e. an electric current.
items.misconception: The metal in the wire is naturally magnetic.
items.random_wrong: The voltage across the wire creates magnetism.
items.why: Students think magnetism is intrinsic to the wire material rather than a consequence of charge motion.

items.q: What are typical units for the Hubble constant?
items.correct: km per second per megaparsec (km/s/Mpc) — recession speed per unit distance.
items.misconception: Metres per second — it's the speed at which the universe expands.
items.random_wrong: Light-years — it's a measure of cosmic distance.
items.why: Students treat H0 as a speed or distance rather than a rate of expansion (speed per unit distance), missing the structure of Hubble's law.

items.q: Is the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies a true Doppler shift?
items.correct: No — it arises from the expansion of space stretching photon wavelengths during transit, not from the galaxy's motion through space.
items.misconception: Yes — it is the same Doppler shift as a moving siren on Earth.
items.random_wrong: No — it is caused by dust in space absorbing blue light.
items.why: Students learn the Doppler analogy early and don't update to the more accurate metric-expansion picture in cosmology.

items.q: Was the Big Bang an explosion of matter into pre-existing space?
items.correct: No — it was the expansion of space itself, beginning from a hot dense state; there was no 'outside' for matter to expand into.
items.misconception: Yes — it was a giant explosion at a point, and galaxies are still flying outwards from it.
items.random_wrong: Yes — it was a bomb-like event that destroyed an earlier universe.
items.why: Pop-science imagery of a fiery explosion encourages the wrong mental model; in fact every point in the universe was once 'the centre'.

items.q: What is the currently accepted age of the universe?
items.correct: Approximately 13.8 billion years (Gyr).
items.misconception: About 14 billion years — close enough for any purpose.
items.random_wrong: About 6 billion years, the same as the Earth.
items.why: Students round aggressively and don't appreciate that the precision (13.8, not 14) reflects measurements from the cosmic microwave background.

items.q: Why do more massive stars have shorter main-sequence lifetimes than smaller ones?
items.correct: They burn through their hydrogen fuel much faster because higher core temperature drives nuclear fusion at a vastly greater rate.
items.misconception: All stars live the same time on the main sequence regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: More massive stars live longer because they have more fuel.
items.why: Students assume more fuel always means longer life, missing that mass increases consumption rate even more sharply than fuel supply.

items.q: Do all stars end their lives as supernovae?
items.correct: No — only sufficiently massive stars (above about 8 solar masses) explode as supernovae; lower-mass stars become white dwarfs.
items.misconception: Yes — every star eventually explodes.
items.random_wrong: Yes — but only after billions of years on Earth's scale.
items.why: Students learn 'star death' as one storyline and don't separate the different end states based on initial mass.

items.q: How does the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depend on light intensity?
items.correct: It does not — max KE depends only on the frequency of the light; intensity affects the rate (number) of electrons emitted.
items.misconception: Higher intensity gives photoelectrons more kinetic energy.
items.random_wrong: Intensity has no effect on anything in the photoelectric effect.
items.why: Students confuse 'brighter light' with 'more energetic photons'; intensity gives more photons, but each one's energy is set by frequency.

items.q: What is the threshold frequency in the photoelectric effect?
items.correct: The minimum frequency of light required to eject electrons from a given metal — it equals the work function divided by Planck's constant.
items.misconception: The minimum intensity of light needed to eject electrons.
items.random_wrong: The frequency at which photons travel at the speed of light.
items.why: Students substitute 'intensity' for 'frequency' or describe threshold as a brightness limit, missing the quantum (per-photon) condition.

items.q: When does an electron behave as a wave?
items.correct: In experiments that probe wave properties (e.g. diffraction through a crystal); in particle-like experiments it behaves as a particle.
items.misconception: Electrons are always waves and only look like particles at low energy.
items.random_wrong: Electrons are sometimes waves and sometimes particles depending on the temperature of the apparatus.
items.why: Students seek a single 'true' description; the modern view is that the experimental context selects which behaviour is observed.

items.q: What determines the de Broglie wavelength of a particle?
items.correct: Its momentum (lambda = h/p) — heavier or faster particles have shorter de Broglie wavelengths.
items.misconception: Its kinetic energy alone, with no dependence on mass.
items.random_wrong: Its electric charge.
items.why: Students think the wavelength is a fixed property of the particle type, missing that it depends on momentum and so on speed and mass together.

items.q: What does specific latent heat measure?
items.correct: The energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing its temperature.
items.misconception: The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 degree.
items.random_wrong: The temperature at which a substance melts or boils.
items.why: Students confuse specific latent heat with specific heat capacity because both involve 'heat' and 'kg', missing the no-temperature-change aspect of latent heat.

items.q: Is the internal energy of a system the same as the heat it contains?
items.correct: No — a system doesn't 'contain heat'; internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of its particles, changed by heating or doing work.
items.misconception: Yes — internal energy is just the stored heat in the object.
items.random_wrong: No — internal energy refers only to the chemical potential energy.
items.why: Students treat heat as a substance stored in objects rather than a process of energy transfer, blurring the heat/internal energy distinction.

items.q: In the ideal gas equation pV = nRT, what must the temperature T be measured in?
items.correct: Kelvin — the absolute temperature scale.
items.misconception: Either Celsius or Kelvin gives the same result.
items.random_wrong: Whatever units make the numbers work out neatly.
items.why: Students plug in Celsius because that's their everyday scale, leading to nonsensical results at low temperatures where the difference is dramatic.

items.q: Why does a metal surface feel colder than a wooden surface in the same room?
items.correct: Metal conducts heat away from your hand more quickly than wood, so it removes thermal energy faster — even though both are at room temperature.
items.misconception: The metal is actually colder than the wood.
items.random_wrong: The metal absorbs the heat from the air, leaving it colder than wood.
items.why: Students equate 'feeling cold' with 'being cold', missing that the sensation is about heat flow rate rather than absolute temperature.

items.q: What is the formula for Young's modulus?
items.correct: Stress / strain — the ratio in the linear (elastic) region of a stress-strain graph.
items.misconception: Force / area — the same as stress itself.
items.random_wrong: Extension / original length — the same as strain.
items.why: Students remember the component words (stress, strain) but mix up that Young's modulus is their ratio, not one of them alone.

items.q: What happens to a metal wire stretched beyond its elastic limit?
items.correct: It deforms plastically and will not return to its original length when the load is removed.
items.misconception: It snaps immediately at the elastic limit.
items.random_wrong: It becomes more elastic and can stretch further.
items.why: Students treat the elastic limit as the breaking point, conflating it with the ultimate tensile strength or fracture point.

items.q: What is the difference between brittle and ductile materials?
items.correct: Brittle materials fracture with little or no plastic deformation; ductile materials undergo significant plastic deformation before breaking.
items.misconception: Brittle materials are weak and ductile materials are strong.
items.random_wrong: Brittle materials are metals and ductile materials are non-metals.
items.why: Students conflate brittleness with weakness, missing that a brittle material (like glass) can have very high tensile strength but no warning before failure.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: Is background radiation always present, even when no source is being studied?
items.correct: Yes — natural sources (rocks, cosmic rays, food) produce a low level of radiation everywhere on Earth.
items.misconception: No — background radiation only appears near nuclear power stations.
items.random_wrong: Yes, but only in cities with old buildings.
items.why: Students associate radiation exclusively with nuclear technology and don't realise natural background dose is ubiquitous.

items.q: Does light always travel at the same speed?
items.correct: Only in a vacuum; in any material medium (water, glass) light travels more slowly, which is what causes refraction.
items.misconception: Yes — c is a universal constant in every situation.
items.random_wrong: No — light always slows with distance from its source.
items.why: Students take 'c is constant' as 'c everywhere' and miss the qualifier 'in a vacuum'.

items.q: What distinguishes gamma rays from X-rays of the same frequency?
items.correct: Origin — gamma rays come from nuclear processes; X-rays come from electron transitions or deceleration of charged particles.
items.misconception: Gamma rays have higher frequency than any X-ray.
items.random_wrong: Gamma rays are slower than X-rays.
items.why: Students distinguish bands by frequency exclusively, missing that gamma and X-ray bands overlap in frequency and are classified by origin.

items.q: In which direction does an electric field point near a positive charge?
items.correct: Radially outwards — the field is defined by the force on a positive test charge, which is repelled.
items.misconception: Radially inwards because positive attracts negative.
items.random_wrong: There is no field around a positive charge unless another charge is present.
items.why: Students confuse the convention (positive test charge) and forget that an isolated positive source still has a field even without another charge.

items.q: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. Is it accelerating?
items.correct: Yes — its direction is constantly changing, so its velocity changes; centripetal acceleration points toward the centre of the circle.
items.misconception: No — constant speed means no acceleration.
items.random_wrong: Only if it speeds up or slows down on the circle.
items.why: Students equate acceleration with speeding up, missing that any change in velocity (including direction) constitutes acceleration.

items.q: Why do atoms emit light only at specific wavelengths rather than a continuous spectrum?
items.correct: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels; transitions between levels emit photons of specific frequencies (E = hf).
items.misconception: Atoms can emit any wavelength, but only certain ones are visible.
items.random_wrong: Atoms emit only one colour each, determined by their temperature.
items.why: Students don't internalise the quantisation of energy levels; without it, line spectra look arbitrary instead of a direct consequence of quantum mechanics.

items.q: If you double your distance from a point source of waves, what happens to the intensity?
items.correct: It falls to one quarter — intensity follows an inverse-square law with distance from a point source.
items.misconception: It halves — intensity is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged because the wave doesn't lose energy.
items.why: Students default to linear scaling instead of the inverse-square law that arises because the same energy spreads over a sphere of growing surface area.

items.q: What is the difference between energy and power?
items.correct: Energy is measured in joules; power is the rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (joules per second).
items.misconception: Energy and power are the same — both describe how much fuel something uses.
items.random_wrong: Power is the energy used over the lifetime of a device.
items.why: Students use 'power' in everyday language to mean 'a lot of energy', missing that power has the dimension of energy per time.
